headlines.geopolitics: Unclaimed Navy veteran given full military honors as community refuses to let him be buried alone
headlines.economy: Deputy AG announces new DOJ fraud division targeting billions in stolen taxpayer funds
headlines.domestic: Fox News Poll finds voters expect AI transformation but don't see it yet in daily life

metadata.daily_do: Look up your local veterans organization and see if they need volunteers for honor guard services

clips.caption: When Duke accidentally becomes wholesome about veterans
Pringle drops historical knowledge about AI panic
Jax finds the one detail everyone missed about office chair procurement

script.text: The telephone was going to destroy human conversation forever. Television would turn our children into zombies. And now artificial intelligence is going to— well, apparently help the government find office chairs.
Good morning, I'm Leo Barros and this is The Friction. It's Monday, March 10th. Let me get you caught up on what happened while you were sleeping off the weekend.
First, geopolitics. In Nashville, hundreds of people showed up to bury Navy veteran Lonnie Wayman with full military honors. He was listed as 'unclaimed' because no family came forward.
Good.
A VA representative said quote, 'unclaimed does not mean forgotten,' and that the community had effectively claimed him themselves.
That's— that actually made me tear up a little reading it this morning.
Wait, they released doves at the end? At a cemetery?
Moving to the economy. Deputy AG Todd Blanche announced a new DOJ fraud division targeting widespread Medicaid and federal program fraud.
He cited a case where two guys from Philadelphia drove to Minneapolis, set up fake homeless shelters, and walked away with four million in federal funds.
Four million? For homeless shelters that didn't exist?
I mean, the fact that it was that easy is kind of the problem.
The new policy will prosecute any fraud over one thousand and one dollars as a felony. Quote, 'no fraud too small, no fraud too large.'
One thousand and one? Why not just make it an even thousand? Did they think fraudsters were gonna be like, 'Oh well, I was gonna steal a thousand but since it's only a misdemeanor...'
And domestically, a new Fox News poll shows eighty-seven percent of voters think AI will significantly change American life, but only forty-seven percent find it useful right now.
That tracks. Everyone's talking about it, nobody's actually using it.
Eighty-nine percent want disclosure requirements for AI-generated content. And seventy-four percent of Americans think life exists or existed on other planets.
script.direction: slight amusement on the last part

script.text: Hold up, we just jumped from AI regulation to aliens? Was that in the same poll?
Apparently Fox News throws in a random alien question now. I'm not complaining.
Let's dig into this veteran story because it's hitting me harder than I expected. We talk a lot about government failing people, but this feels different.
Lonnie Wayman served his country. He dies, no family shows up, and the state labels him 'unclaimed.' But then hundreds of strangers show up to make sure he gets buried with honors.
It's beautiful, but it's also an indictment, right? Like, where was the system that should have been tracking this guy?
Sometimes the system is the community. Maybe that's not a failure, maybe that's how it's supposed to work.
But Duke, come on. We spend seven hundred billion on defense, and we can't keep track of the people who served?
We spend a lot on veteran services too. Sometimes people slip through cracks. It happens in every system.
Slip through cracks? This man died alone and nobody knew about it until—
But he didn't get buried alone. That's the point.
Okay, but let me ask this. Should we be relying on the kindness of strangers, or should there be a system in place?
There should absolutely be a system. The VA should have case workers tracking isolated veterans. We should have—
Hold on. You want federal case workers checking on every veteran to make sure they have friends?
I want a system that doesn't let people who served their country die forgotten. Is that controversial?
He wasn't forgotten. Hundreds of people showed up.
Can I just say, the fact that we're arguing about whether the government should care if veterans die alone is kind of proving Bree's point?
I'm not saying the government shouldn't care. I'm saying maybe community caring is better than government caring.
Why can't we have both? Why is it either-or?
Because government programs create dependency. Community creates connection.
Oh my God, dependency? The man is dead, Duke. What's he gonna be dependent on?
I mean while he was alive. If there's always a government case worker, people stop checking on their neighbors.
But Duke, wasn't this guy isolated precisely because there wasn't community connection?
Maybe. But look what happened when people found out. They showed up. That's America.
It's America cleaning up after America failed.
Or it's America working exactly like it should. People taking care of people.
I'm with Duke on this one. The government can't hug people. It can write checks, but it can't show up to your funeral.
But it can make sure there's a funeral to show up to.
Here's what I keep thinking about. The VA rep said Lonnie wasn't actually unclaimed because the community claimed him. But they only found out about him after he was already gone.
That's fair. Maybe the system is figuring out how to connect those things earlier.
Exactly. And that's not about creating dependency, it's about creating awareness.
Okay, awareness I can get behind. But let's not turn it into another federal bureaucracy.
Plot twist: Duke and Bree agree on something. Someone check the temperature in hell.
And speaking of bureaucracy, let's talk about that DOJ fraud announcement, because four million dollars for fake homeless shelters is insane.
It is insane. But you know what's also insane? That we're so focused on punishing fraud that we might make it harder for real programs to get funding.
Are you seriously defending fraud right now?
No, I'm saying that when you make the penalty for any fraud over a thousand dollars a felony, you create a system where—
Where people don't commit fraud. That's the point.
Or where legitimate organizations spend more money on compliance than on actually helping people.
Good. Maybe they should have been spending that money on compliance all along.
But Bree has a point about the chilling effect. If you make the penalties too harsh, organizations might just avoid federal programs altogether.
Or maybe we stop giving away money like candy, which is what the Deputy AG said we're doing.
Wait, did he literally say we give money away like candy? Because that's the most honest thing a government official has said in years.
He did. He also said there are daycare centers getting federal funding that don't exist.
Phantom daycares. Fake homeless shelters. At some point you have to ask, is anyone actually checking these applications?
That's a staffing and oversight problem, not a penalty problem. You can't prosecute your way out of administrative failure.
But you can deter it. If word gets out that fraud means prison time, fewer people try it.
Or you just catch the dumb criminals and miss the smart ones who know how to game a system that's focused on punishment instead of prevention.
Alright, I'm gonna step in here because we're about to start arguing about criminal justice theory, and I need to get Professor Pringle on the record.
Thank you, Leo. This story about Lonnie Wayman reminds me of something quite remarkable from 1921.
script.direction: settling in to tell a story

script.text: After World War One, there were thousands of unidentified American soldiers buried in makeshift graves across Europe. The government wanted to honor them, but how do you honor someone no one can name?
So they created the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier at Arlington. One unidentified soldier would represent all unidentified soldiers. The entire nation would claim him.
That's actually beautiful.
The ceremony on November 11th, 1921 drew over a hundred thousand people to Washington. President Harding placed the Medal of Honor on the tomb.
But here's what strikes me about Mr. Wayman's story. In 1921, it took an act of Congress and the full weight of government to honor unknown soldiers.
In 2026, it took a few phone calls and some Facebook posts to get hundreds of people to show up in Nashville. The mechanism changed, but the impulse is identical.
That's a really good point, Doc. Maybe the system isn't broken, it's just decentralized now.
Precisely. Americans have always believed that military service creates a debt that outlasts death. We've just found new ways to pay it.
Professor, you just made me feel patriotic about a Facebook event. That's impressive.
Social media is just the telegraph with better graphics, Jax. The message remains the same.
Professor, what about this fraud situation? Any historical parallels for government programs being systematically defrauded?
Oh, goodness yes. During the New Deal, the Works Progress Administration lost millions to fraudulent projects. Phantom construction sites, fake work crews.
Roosevelt's response was to create more oversight agencies, which themselves became targets for fraud. It's rather like trying to solve a leak by adding more pipes.
So what worked?
World War Two. Hard to defraud a program when the entire economy is mobilized for war production. Everyone's watching everything.
So the solution to fraud is... total war?
The solution is shared stakes, Miss Aldana. When everyone has skin in the game, fraud becomes much more difficult.
The problem with our current system is that the people approving the funds rarely see the consequences of approving bad applications.
That's exactly right. It's not their money, so they don't care if it gets stolen.
In 1943, a government clerk in Baltimore caught a fraudulent steel contract because he knew his brother-in-law worked at that factory. Personal knowledge prevented fraud.
So we need government workers to have more brother-in-laws? I can get behind that jobs program.
We need government workers to have stakes in the communities they serve, yes. Remote bureaucracy is an invitation to fraud.
That's actually a great segue to our AI story, because remote everything seems to be the direction we're heading.
Alright, everybody, it's time for Rapid Fire, where we talk about the news that's too weird for the front page but too good to ignore.
First up: Palantir, the company that makes AI for the military, is now using that same technology to find office chairs for government employees.
I'm sorry, what?
Yeah, the same artificial intelligence that tracks terrorists is now optimizing federal furniture procurement. Your tax dollars at work, folks.
I mean, if it saves money on office supplies, I'm not gonna complain.
Bree, this is the same company that helped hunt down Osama bin Laden. And now they're like, 'You know what, let's tackle lumbar support.'
During World War Two, IBM used punch cards to track military supplies. Same principle, different century.
Professor, you just compared office chair AI to defeating the Nazis. I don't know if that's inspiring or terrifying.
Next story: A boss in Ireland repeatedly shouted 'potato' at his Irish employee and it's now a workplace harassment case.
Oh no.
The employee is suing, saying it was ethnic harassment. The boss claims he was just... really excited about potatoes?
There's no good explanation for repeatedly shouting 'potato' at someone.
This is why we need workplace harassment training. Like, basic human decency training.
I love that somewhere there's a lawyer who had to argue, with a straight face, whether shouting 'potato' constitutes ethnic harassment.
Moving on: Louisiana classrooms have until Friday to post the Ten Commandments, and apparently some schools are just... not gonna do it.
This is gonna end up in federal court so fast.
Good. Maybe we can finally settle this church-state thing once and for all.
Duke, we've been trying to settle this for like two hundred years.
The Establishment Clause has been litigated more than any other part of the Constitution. It's our most successful industry.
And finally, a Florida drug dealer locked a guy in a storage unit for months over an unpaid debt. The guy survived on gas station snacks and rainwater.
How do you lock someone in a storage unit for months? Don't those places have security?
This is Florida, Duke. The security guard was probably the drug dealer's cousin.
I'm sorry, how is this not kidnapping? Like, first-degree, federal kidnapping?
Oh, it is. The dealer got arrested. But somewhere there's a storage unit company that needs to explain how they didn't notice a guy living in unit B-47 for three months.
I have so many questions about this story that I don't want answered.
That's Florida for you. Always raising questions you didn't know you had.
Alright, let me bring us back to earth with today's Daily Do.
Lonnie Wayman's story got me thinking about all the veterans in our communities who might be isolated. Not just the ones who are dying, but the ones who are living alone.
So here's what you can do today: Look up your local veterans organization. VFW, American Legion, Vietnam Veterans of America. See if they need volunteers for honor guard services.
Honor guard isn't just for funerals. They do ceremonies, flag presentations, community events. It's a way to make sure veterans know their service is remembered.
That's a good one, Leo. My dad does honor guard in Indianapolis. He says it's the most meaningful thing he's ever volunteered for.
And if you can't do honor guard, most of these organizations need help with other stuff. Driving people to appointments, organizing events, just showing up to meetings.
Or if you're not ready to volunteer, just... check in on the veterans in your life. A phone call matters.
The point is, we don't have to wait for someone to be labeled 'unclaimed' to claim them. We can do that while they're alive.
As Harry Truman said, 'Our debt to the heroic men and valiant women in the service of our country can never be repaid. They have earned our undying gratitude.'
Gratitude, however, is not passive. It requires action.
Professor Pringle with the wisdom, Duke getting emotional about honor guard, Bree pushing for systemic change, and Leo making us all better citizens.
I'm Jax Mooney, and this has been The Friction. We'll see you tomorrow when we'll probably argue about something completely different but somehow end up in the same place.
That's the show. Thanks for listening.
